criteria.description: The mascot's body is a single rounded box — a rounded cube reading slightly taller than wide, approximately 1:1.2 (width:height). All corners and edges are generously radiused, never sharp; the top edge and the vertical side edges curve. There is no separate head: the face sits directly on the upper-front face of the box. This silhouette is the load-bearing recognizability cue at shoulder-companion scale — not a sphere, not a humanoid figure, not a tall upright box. A frame that renders the mascot as a ball, a cylinder, a sharp-cornered cube, or anything with a distinct head-and-body break is a hard identity violation.

The mascot reads at shoulder-companion scale — roughly the size of an adult human head. In the canonical two-shot with Sean (A-7), the box height is approximately equal to Sean's head height as it perches on his shoulder. Any Phase 5/6 frame that renders the mascot at hand-size (too small to read as a companion) or at torso-size or larger (reading as furniture or a second figure) relative to Sean is a scale violation. In standalone frames the box should still read as a small creature you could pick up, not a piece of architecture.

Exactly two short, rounded, cylindrical ear/arm nubs project horizontally from the left and right faces of the box, set near the box's vertical mid-height (roughly on the construction midline). Each nub is short — about one-fifth of the box width in length — with a rounded blunt end and no articulation. They are not arms and bear no hands, fingers, joints, or detail. Both nubs read in front, three-quarter, and back views; in pure side/profile only the near nub reads. A nub rendered as an arm, a hand, a wing, a flipper, or a detailed ear is a violation.

In the SIDE and three-quarter views, the near ear/arm nub is seen end-on and reads as a small circular disc (an end-cap) on the body's side face — the C-1 SIDE-view 'swirl'. This circle IS the nub viewed down its axis, not a separate feature and not stray construction: it is never a navel, a port, an emblem, a button, or a logo, and it must never be 'cleaned away' as a construction artifact. Conversely, no NEW circular feature may be invented on the body's faces — the only circle that belongs there is the end-on nub in side/three-quarter framings. A side view that drops the near-nub end-cap, or that adds an unexplained disc/emblem to a face, drifts the character.

Exactly four short stub legs sit beneath the box, set close together under the body's footprint rather than splayed wide. Each leg is a short rounded peg — about one-sixth of the box height — with no feet, toes, joints, or paw detail. In front view the front pair reads clearly with the back pair partly occluded behind; in side/profile the near front and near back legs read. A two-legged biped stance, wide-splayed legs, three or five legs, or detailed feet is a violation.

The mascot has no arms and no hands, ever. The two ear/arm nubs never extend into limbs, never bend, and never grasp, hold, point, or gesture with anything. The creature interacts with the world by leaning, tilting, and perching — not by reaching. Any frame that grows an arm, a hand, fingers, or a held object off the nubs is a hard identity violation. This negative-space rule is the most common drift risk: a generator asked to make the mascot 'do' something in a scene will reach for an arm, and it must not.

The mascot has no hair, ever. The top and all faces of the rounded box are bare and unbroken — no hair, tuft, topknot, cowlick, fur, spikes, antennae, or any growth sprouts from the box, and nothing breaks the clean top edge of the silhouette. The only marks on the box surface are the face features on the upper-front face (two dot eyes, brows, construction cross-line, minimal mouth) and the cross-hatch shadow. A frame that adds a hair tuft, a sprig on top, or any hair-like growth is a hard identity violation — this is a common generator drift (it reads the box-top as a head and reaches for hair) and must be held.

The face carries exactly two simple dot/oval eyes on the upper-front face of the box, each capped by a short curved brow stroke a small gap above it. The eyes are small ovals taller than wide, in warm graphite, sitting symmetric about the vertical construction centerline and roughly one eye-width apart at their inner edges, riding on the horizontal construction midline. The eyes plus brows carry the entire emotional read — there is no large mouth to emote with. A single eye, three or more eyes, large round cartoon eyes with big glossy highlights, or eyes lacking the brow stroke drift the character.

A faint pencil construction cross-line stays visible on the front face beneath the features: a horizontal midline running through the eyes and a soft vertical centerline bisecting the face. The cross-line reads at roughly half the weight of the eye and contour line and is never cleaned away — it is the pencil-test register's construction signature carried onto the creature, and it also locates the eyes and the nubs. A face rendered with no construction cross-line (a clean digital-finish look) is a register violation.

At most a small, short mouth line sits low on the front face beneath and between the eyes — a single understated graphite stroke, never a feature. The mascot has no large mouth: no open-mouth shapes, no teeth, no lips, no smile or frown wide enough to carry expression. Expression lives in the eyes, the brows, and head tilt, never in the mouth. A grinning, gaping, talking, or emoting mouth is a violation. (Tagged continuity, not identity_critical, because the mouth reads inconsistently across references — see cy-confidence-notes.md.)

The body fills with a warm terracotta/clay orange (#C77C52, ±10 RGB) — a muted, medium-saturation clay tone. It is not red, not bright pumpkin or safety orange, and specifically not the saturated synthetic orange of the retired pixel-art mascot. The fill is flat color with cross-hatched warm-graphite shadow on the far and under planes (reading as the darker clay tone #9E5A38), never gradient- or cel-shaded. The terracotta body is the mascot's primary color-identity cue alongside the rounded-box silhouette.

Contour and interior marks are warm-graphite pencil line (#3D3530, never pure black, never cool gray), drawn at varied HB-to-2B weight (~0.5-2px) so the drawing reads as hand-drawn. Shadow on the body is cross-hatching — warm graphite over the terracotta fill, reading as the darker clay tone #9E5A38 — laid on the far side plane and the underside, following the box's form. It is never a flat tonal fill, never cel-shading, never an airbrush gradient. This is the identical line and shadow treatment as the sean-anchor Bible: the mascot must read in the same pencil-test medium as Sean, since they share the frame in Act 2.

The mascot sits on the shared warm cream paper substrate (#F2E6CC) with visible grain, and casts a soft, loose graphite-scribble shadow (~#B3A78C) directly beneath the box on the paper. The cast shadow is a hand-drawn scribbled ellipse, not a rendered drop-shadow and not a hard vector shape. Production marks (hole-punch, sheet label) live on the sheet as in the rest of the register. A flat vector cream rectangle with no grain, or a rendered soft-box shadow, is a register violation. Shared with the T1 HF02 paper-texture gate, stated here for downstream consumers who do not run T1.

In two-shots with Sean, the canonical pairing (A-7) is the mascot perched on Sean's shoulder, the box leaning lightly toward his jaw and cheek, nubs and legs tucked against him. This is the Act 2 two-character continuity ground-truth for scale and placement: the mascot rides the shoulder rather than floating beside Sean or standing on the ground at his feet, unless a beat explicitly calls for it. The rule governs scale and placement continuity — not a specific frame's staging, which is Bea and Flo's call — and gives Em and Annie a citable invariant for Act 2 two-shots.

Alarm reads through the eyes and brows ONLY: the two small graphite dot eyes open wide (still small dots, never glossy cartoon eyes), brows raised high above them, mouth a small open shape. No hair or tuft appears on the box-top. Full terracotta color on cream paper.

Delight reads as happy upward-arched (crescent) eyes with raised brows and a small open smile. The arched eyes are the expression's squint, not a change of eye style; no hair or tuft on the box-top; full terracotta color on cream paper, warm-graphite line and cross-hatch.

Sleep reads as closed eyes drawn as two small downward graphite arcs riding the construction midline, brows soft, mouth a tiny relaxed line. The closed-eye arcs are the sleep state of the two dot eyes, not a new eye style. No hair or tuft on the box-top; full terracotta color on cream paper.

Alert-perk reads as the two ear-arm nubs projecting upward and forward (perked) with focused small dot eyes and lifted brows. The nubs perk; the eyes stay small graphite dots, never enlarged glossy cartoon eyes; no hair or tuft on the box-top; full terracotta color on cream paper.

The idle/settle loop is a moving hold: the rounded box breathes by sinking and softening, then rising and lengthening, on an eased (never linear) curve. Volume is conserved — as the box lowers it widens slightly, as it rises it narrows (scale_x × scale_y ≈ 1) — and identity holds unchanged across the loop. The legs stay gathered close and the nubs stay neutral through the breath; one nub may lag the other by a frame for life. A linear/floaty breath, a volume-breaking squash, or any identity shift across the loop is a motion violation.

The curiosity look is a WHOLE-BODY cant, not a head turn — the creature has no neck, so the entire box leans ~15° and the construction cross-line cants with the box; the box-top never reads as a head tilting on a body. The two dot eyes lead the cant (eye-lead: the eyes reach the target before the body leans). The two nubs are offset, not twinned — the near nub rises with the cant while the far nub holds or drops. A head-on-neck tilt, twinned nubs, or eyes that follow rather than lead the body is a motion violation.

The perch-settle reads weight through squash and the cast shadow: the box stretches on contact, compresses on impact while the cast shadow tightens and darkens, overshoots, then settles — leaning lightly toward Sean's jaw at shoulder-companion scale (the canonical pairing). The four legs splay only transiently at impact and regather close by the settle; they never hold a wide, splayed, or spidery stance at rest. A weightless landing, a permanently wide stance, or a settle that floats free of the shoulder is a motion violation.

Alert-perk is a fast snap into a long hold: a quick anticipation dip, then the box snaps upright and tall with the two ear/arm nubs perking up-and-forward and the dot eyes widening — but the eyes stay small graphite dots, never enlarging into glossy cartoon eyes, and the nubs perk SHORT and forward, never extending or flaring into raised arms. A slow alert, glossy/enlarged eyes, or nubs that read as arms is a motion violation.

The hop follows a clean parabolic arc with Odd-Rule gravity spacing (wide low, tightening toward the apex) and a Fourth-Down hang at the top. The box squashes on the crouch and the landing and stretches on takeoff, volume conserved; the four stub legs trail in the air and regather close on landing, never becoming springy or jointed limbs. In the side/profile keys the near ear/arm nub reads as a small end-cap disc on the side face — never a large circle, navel, or emblem. Linear/even airborne spacing, springy legs, or an oversized side disc is a motion violation.

Sleep-settle is the slowest motion — a long ease-in with no snap: the box slumps lower and softer as the eyes lower toward the construction midline and the nubs droop, settling into sleep with the eyes closed as two small downward graphite arcs riding the midline (the closed state of the dot eyes, not a new eye style). A fast/snappy sleep, or closed eyes drawn as a new shape (X's, lashes), is a motion violation.

Across every frame of every motion the no-arms rule holds (reinforces IR.claude-mascot.anatomy.no-arms-no-hands for motion specifically): the two ear/arm nubs may lag, trail, droop, or perk for follow-through and acting, but they NEVER extend into arms, bend, reach, grasp, point, or balance like limbs, and no hand or held object ever grows off them. Any frame in any cycle that grows an arm or hand off the nubs is a hard identity violation.
